The company’s Financial Framework targets 5 to 7 % comparable revenue growth per year; using the 5 % midpoint, sales rise from €75.9 bn in FY-2024 to c. €97 bn in FY-2029, which is approximately a 5 % CAGR. data.title: Is It Too Late To Consider Centrus Energy (LEU) After The Recent Share Price Pullback?
data.description:
• This article examines whether Centrus Energy, at around US$199 per share, is still priced attractively or whether most of the easy gains may already have occurred, by exploring what the current share price might imply about value.
  • The stock has been volatile recently, with a 24.4% decline over the past week and a 39.8% pullback over the last 30 days. This comes even though the 1 year return stands at 74.7% and the 3 year return is approximately 4x.
  • Recent moves have put fresh attention on Centrus Energy, as investors consider how the current share price compares with longer term return figures, such as the 26.9% year to date decline and the very large 5 year gains of around 7x. This combination of sharp recent declines and strong multi year returns has raised questions about whether expectations or perceived risk around the stock have shifted.
  • Centrus Energy currently records a valuation score of 1 out of 6 on our checks. Next, we will look at what different valuation approaches indicate about the stock today and then conclude with a broader way to think about its value that goes beyond any single metric.

Centrus Energy scores just 1/6 on our valuation checks. See what other red flags we found in the full valuation breakdown.

Advertisement

Approach 1: Centrus Energy Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model estimates what a company might be worth by projecting its future cash flows and then discounting those amounts back to today, using a required rate of return. It is essentially asking what those future dollars are worth in present terms.

For Centrus Energy, the model used is a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach, based on cash flows available to shareholders. The latest twelve month Free Cash Flow is about $41.6 million. Analyst and extrapolated projections show Free Cash Flow moving through a mix of negative and positive years, with a projected $176 million in 2030, all expressed in US$.

Bringing these projected cash flows back to today gives an estimated intrinsic value of about $214.66 per share. Compared with the current share price of around $199, the model implies the stock is about 7.2% undervalued. This is a relatively small gap and suggests the market price is fairly close to this DCF estimate.

Result: ABOUT RIGHT

Centrus Energy is fairly valued according to our Discounted Cash Flow (DCF), but this can change at a moment's notice. Track the value in your watchlist or portfolio and be alerted on when to act.

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for Centrus Energy.

Approach 2: Centrus Energy Price vs Earnings

For a profitable company, the P/E ratio is a useful way to think about what you are paying for each dollar of earnings. It ties directly to the bottom line and lets you compare how the market prices those earnings across different businesses.

What counts as a “normal” P/E depends on how the market views growth potential and risk. Higher expected growth or lower perceived risk can support a higher P/E, while slower expected growth or higher risk usually point to a lower, more conservative multiple.

Centrus Energy currently trades on a P/E of about 50.35x. That is above the Oil and Gas industry average of around 14.46x and also higher than the peer group average of roughly 44.06x. Simply Wall St’s “Fair Ratio” for Centrus Energy is 13.10x. This Fair Ratio is a proprietary estimate of the P/E that might be reasonable given factors such as earnings growth characteristics, industry, profit margins, market cap and specific risks.

Compared with simple peer or industry comparisons, the Fair Ratio aims to be more tailored, because it adjusts for those company specific factors instead of assuming all businesses deserve similar multiples. With Centrus Energy’s actual P/E well above its Fair Ratio, the shares screen as expensive on this measure.

Result: OVERVALUED

P/E ratios tell one story, but what if the real opportunity lies elsewhere? Start investing in legacies, not executives. Discover our 23 top founder-led companies.

Upgrade Your Decision Making: Choose your Centrus Energy Narrative

Earlier we mentioned that there is an even better way to understand valuation. On Simply Wall St you can use Narratives on the Centrus Energy Community page to link your view of the company’s story to a forecast and a fair value, then compare that fair value to the current price. The system updates your Narrative automatically as new news or earnings arrive. This is why one investor might build a bullish Centrus Narrative around a fair value of US$390.00, while another takes a more cautious view closer to US$171.40. You can then quickly see which version of the story you think makes more sense for you.

For Centrus Energy, however, we'll make it really easy for you with previews of two leading Centrus Energy Narratives:

First up is a bullish view that leans on supportive policy, federal funding and long term nuclear demand, and then a more cautious take that focuses on contract concentration, dilution and funding uncertainty. Seeing both side by side can help you decide which set of assumptions feels closer to your own.

🐂 Centrus Energy Bull Case

Fair value in this bullish narrative: US$279.73 per share

Implied discount to this fair value at the last close of US$199.19: about 29% undervalued

Revenue growth assumption: 11.05% a year

  • Analysts in this camp anchor their view on robust policy support for nuclear, growing enrichment demand and Centrus Energy's position as a key supplier across LEU and HALEU.
  • They factor in revenue growing at double digit rates while margins step down from current levels but remain healthy enough to support a higher earnings base by around 2028.
  • This narrative leans on access to capital, Department of Energy funding, a US$3.6b backlog and operating leverage from expansion to support the case that the stock can justify a relatively high future P/E multiple.

🐻 Centrus Energy Bear Case

Fair value in this bearish narrative: US$171.40 per share

Implied premium to this fair value at the last close of US$199.19: about 16% overvalued

Revenue growth assumption: 17.98% annual decline

  • This more cautious view leans on contract concentration, slower assumed revenue growth, shrinking margins and the risk that nuclear demand could be pressured by alternative energy and technology shifts.
  • Analysts here build in much lower earnings by 2028, continued share count growth of 7.0% a year and a need for a very high future P/E to support even the lower price target of US$108.00.
  • Funding and dilution from the US$1b at the market program, uncertainty around the size and timing of future Department of Energy awards and regulatory or ESG constraints are central to this narrative.

These Narratives frame the spread of expectations that is already in the market, from higher growth and sustained policy support through to slower growth and more pressure on returns. Your job as an investor is to decide which earnings path, margin profile and funding outcome feels more realistic for Centrus Energy, and where you would be comfortable owning the stock relative to those assumptions.

data.title: Kalmar Oyj (HLSE:KALMAR) Net Margin At 8.6% Tests Bullish Profitability Narratives
data.description:
Kalmar Oyj (HLSE:KALMAR) has put up another solid print for FY 2025, with Q3 revenue at €435.6 million and EPS of €0.70, while the trailing twelve month figures sit at €1.7 billion in revenue and EPS of €2.26. The company has seen quarterly revenue move from €416.5 million in Q2 2024 to €435.6 million in Q3 2025, with EPS stepping from €0.49 to €0.70 over the same run, giving investors a clear view of how the top and bottom lines are tracking through the year. With a trailing net margin of 8.6% and the share price around €44.58, the latest results keep the focus squarely on how durable these margins look from here.

See our full analysis for Kalmar Oyj.

With the numbers on the table, the next step is to see how they stack up against the prevailing stories around Kalmar, and which of those narratives hold up when you line them against the recent trends in growth and profitability.

See what the community is saying about Kalmar Oyj

Advertisement

Margins Steady Around 8.6% Net

  • Over the last 12 months, Kalmar’s net income of €145 million on €1.7b of revenue works out to an 8.6% net margin, slightly above the 8.1% margin reported a year earlier.
  • Bulls point out that this margin profile lines up with several supportive trends, yet the numbers still leave room for debate:
    • On the bullish side, services now carry an 18.5% margin and 34% of sales, and programs like Driving Excellence are targeting €50 million in annualised efficiencies. Investors arguing the bullish case see this as helpful for sustaining or lifting margins.
    • At the same time, tariffs on U.S. bound equipment and batteries plus price increases of 5% to 15% create the risk that cost pressure or customer pushback eventually eats into that 8.6% net level. This gives bears something concrete to point to when they question how durable these margins are.
Kalmar’s recent margin print is what bullish investors often like to see before talking about higher earnings potential, yet the tariff and pricing backdrop means the story is far from one sided. This is why some bulls still frame this as an early innings margin story rather than a finished one. 🐂 Kalmar Oyj Bull Case

Earnings Trend Breaks From 5 Year Decline

  • Trailing earnings growth over the last year is 0.5%, compared with a 13.8% per year decline over the past five years, and Q3 net income of €44.7 million sits above the €31.2 million recorded in Q2 2024.
  • Bears argue that a single year of modest growth does not fully answer questions raised by that multi year decline, and the quarterly pattern gives them some data to work with:
    • Q3 2025 earnings per share of €0.70 follow earlier quarters of €0.53 and €0.61 in 2025, but the longer term record still shows that earnings had been shrinking at 13.8% per year. Cautious investors highlight this as evidence that profitability has not yet shown a long multi year rebuild.
    • Even with a DCF fair value of €57.50 and a current price of €44.58, skeptics note that a 0.5% trailing earnings lift on its own is a small shift compared with the past erosion. They look for more than a few quarters of improvement before they fully buy into a lasting turnaround.
That mix of a small positive earnings step against a long history of declines is why more cautious investors still push the bearish angle, even as the recent numbers start to move in a direction they prefer. 🐻 Kalmar Oyj Bear Case

P/E Discount Versus Peers Stands Out

  • Kalmar trades on a trailing P/E of 19.7x at a share price of €44.58, compared with a peer average of 22.5x and a European Machinery industry average of 21.6x, while the DCF fair value is set at €57.50.
  • Supporters of the bullish view say this gap hints at mispricing, but the valuation spread means different things depending on which risks are the focus:
    • On one side, the DCF fair value of €57.50 is above the current price, and forecast earnings growth of 8.7% per year with revenue growth of 5.2% gives bulls a numerical basis to argue the stock trades at a discount to its cash flow potential relative to similar machinery names.
    • On the other, the five year earnings decline of 13.8% per year provides a concrete counterpoint, as more cautious investors see the lower P/E multiple as a reflection of that track record rather than a clear signal that the market has overlooked something material.

Next Steps

To see how these results tie into long-term growth, risks, and valuation, check out the full range of community narratives for Kalmar Oyj on Simply Wall St. Add the company to your watchlist or portfolio so you'll be alerted when the story evolves.

See the numbers differently? If this data points you in another direction, shape your own view in just a few minutes and Do it your way

See What Else Is Out There

Approach 1: American Express Excess Returns Analysis

The Excess Returns model looks at how effectively American Express turns shareholder capital into profits, then compares those profits to the return investors usually expect. The key idea is simple: if the company is earning more on its equity than the required return, that “excess” can support a higher intrinsic value per share.

For American Express, book value is $48.80 per share and the average return on equity is 36.58%. That translates into a stable earnings figure of $20.63 per share, based on weighted future Return on Equity estimates from 13 analysts. Against a cost of equity of $4.67 per share, this leaves an excess return of $15.96 per share, which is what the model treats as value created over and above the required return.

The model also uses a stable book value of $56.41 per share, sourced from weighted future Book Value estimates from 9 analysts, to anchor those excess returns over time. Putting this all together, the Excess Returns model arrives at an estimated intrinsic value of about $383.55 per share, which is around 12.0% above the recent share price of $337.50. On this basis, the shares screen as undervalued on excess returns.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Our Excess Returns analysis suggests American Express is undervalued by 12.0%. Track this in your watchlist or portfolio, or discover 53 more high quality undervalued stocks.

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for American Express.

Approach 2: American Express Price vs Earnings

The P/E ratio is usually the cleanest quick check for profitable companies, because it links what you pay directly to the earnings that each share currently generates. It gives you a sense of how many dollars the market is willing to pay today for one dollar of earnings.

What counts as a “fair” P/E depends on what investors expect from the business and how much risk they see. Higher expected earnings growth or lower perceived risk can justify a higher P/E, while lower growth expectations or higher risk tend to support a lower P/E.

American Express currently trades on a P/E of 21.66x. That is above the Consumer Finance industry average of 8.05x and close to the peer group average of 21.91x. Simply Wall St’s Fair Ratio for American Express is 20.49x. This Fair Ratio is a proprietary estimate of what a reasonable P/E could be, given factors like the company’s earnings profile, industry, margins, size and risk characteristics.

Because the Fair Ratio folds in these company specific drivers, it can be more useful than a simple comparison with peers or the industry as a whole. With the current P/E of 21.66x sitting above the Fair Ratio of 20.49x, the shares screen as slightly expensive on this metric.

Result: OVERVALUED

On Simply Wall St’s Community page you can use Narratives, where you set out your own story for American Express, plug in assumptions for future revenue, earnings, margins and a fair value, see that story turned into a full forecast and valuation that updates automatically when new results or news arrive, then compare your Fair Value with the current price. This allows you to decide for yourself whether the current market price looks high, low or roughly in line with the story you believe.

For American Express however we will make it really easy for you with previews of two leading American Express Narratives:

Implied discount to this fair value versus the recent price of US$337.50: about 4.9% undervalued using ((354.83 - 337.50) / 354.83).

Revenue growth assumption in this storyline: 10.28% a year.

🐻 American Express Bear Case

Implied premium to this fair value versus the recent price of US$337.50: about 9.5% overvalued using ((337.50 - 308.19) / 308.19).

data.title: Kuroda Group (TSE:287A) Margin Improvement Challenges Bearish Earnings Narratives
data.description:

Over recent quarters the company has seen quarterly revenue range from ¥29.6b to ¥31.3b and basic EPS move between ¥10.39 and ¥27.61, while trailing twelve month EPS shifted from ¥55.77 to ¥65.39 as total revenue moved from ¥139.3b to ¥120.4b. For investors, this combination of softer top line and better margins sets up a results season focused firmly on how durable the profitability picture really is.

Margins Improve Despite Softer Sales

  • Over the last 12 months, revenue eased 2.9% while earnings rose 29.1%. This lifted the net profit margin from 1.7% to 2.3% even though the top line softened.
  • What is interesting for a more bullish take is that this higher 2.3% margin is coming alongside quarterly revenue drifting in a tight band of about ¥29.6b to ¥31.3b, which suggests:
    • Supporters of the bullish view can point to EPS on a trailing basis moving from ¥55.77 to ¥65.39 while total revenue moved from ¥139.3b to ¥120.4b. Profitability per share has been higher even as sales have been lower.
    • On the other hand, cautious investors may flag that earnings growth is currently tied to this margin lift during a 2.9% revenue decline, so they will watch closely how that margin behaves if sales pressure continues.

Quarterly EPS Swings Around ¥10 to ¥27

  • Across the recent quarters in the data, basic EPS has moved between roughly ¥10.39 and ¥27.61 while net income for individual quarters ranged from ¥441 million to ¥1,172 million. This shows that profitability per share can vary quite a bit even when quarterly revenue sits around ¥29b to ¥31b.
  • Critics who lean more bearish often focus on this kind of variability, and the numbers here give them both support and pushback:
    • Bears can point out that trailing twelve month EPS has moved from ¥92.20 to ¥65.39 while trailing twelve month net income went from ¥3,914 million to ¥2,776 million, so the most recent 12 month snapshot is lower than an earlier period in the dataset.
    • At the same time, the same dataset also shows earnings 29.1% higher year on year alongside the margin shift from 1.7% to 2.3%, so the latest year still looks stronger than the year before even if it sits below the earlier ¥92.20 EPS peak.

P/E Of 15.7x And DCF Gap

  • Supporters of a more optimistic stance see these valuation figures as important context for the recent earnings profile:
    • Bulls would highlight that the 15.7x P/E sits below both the industry and peer averages even after a 29.1% earnings rise and margin improvement to 2.3%. They view this as a potential disconnect between price and recent profitability.
    • They may also point to the wide gap between the ¥1,029 market price and the ¥2,504.27 DCF fair value, arguing that if earnings and margins stay around current levels, this discount leaves room for sentiment to adjust toward the DCF figure.

Bulls argue that a lower P/E and the wide DCF gap could signal a pricing mismatch for a company that has grown earnings faster than revenue, and if you want to see how that story is built out in full you can check the broader narrative here: 📊 Read the full Kuroda Group Consensus Narrative.

data.title: Greg Abel Backs Kraft Heinz Shift And Signals Berkshire’s Ownership Approach
data.description:
• Greg Abel, CEO of Berkshire Hathaway (NYSE:BRK.B), publicly backed Kraft Heinz’s decision to cancel its previously planned separation.
  • Abel described the shift as a positive turnaround for Kraft Heinz and reaffirmed Berkshire’s commitment to the relationship.
  • The comments highlighted closer alignment between Berkshire and one of its largest consumer holdings, with potential implications for how Berkshire interacts with other portfolio companies.

Berkshire Hathaway sits at the center of several key sectors through its mix of operating businesses and large equity holdings, and Kraft Heinz is one of the most visible consumer brands in that group. Food and beverage companies have been reevaluating product portfolios, cost structures, and brand focus, and Kraft Heinz’s scrapped separation plan fits into that broader rethink. For investors watching NYSE:BRK.B, Abel’s endorsement adds a fresh data point on how Berkshire views long term partnerships with consumer staples holdings.

Abel’s comments also hint at how actively Berkshire’s leadership may weigh in when a core holding considers major structural change. For you as a shareholder or potential investor, this episode can help frame questions around Berkshire’s role as an influential owner and how that might shape decisions at other companies in its portfolio over time.

Stay updated on the most important news stories for Berkshire Hathaway by adding it to your watchlist or portfolio. Alternatively, explore our Community to discover new perspectives on Berkshire Hathaway.

1 thing going right for Berkshire Hathaway that this headline doesn't cover.

For Berkshire Hathaway, Greg Abel’s support for Kraft Heinz’s reversal looks less like a one off comment and more like a signal about how the new CEO wants to run long term capital allocation. By backing management’s decision to keep the group intact, Abel is effectively prioritising operational execution and brand strength at a core consumer holding over transaction driven restructuring. That lines up with Berkshire’s long running preference for patience and limited portfolio churn, where management teams are expected to compound value rather than constantly reconfigure assets.

Advertisement

The Risks and Rewards Investors Should Consider

  • ⚠️ Concentration in a large consumer holding like Kraft Heinz means execution missteps at the portfolio company level can feed through to Berkshire’s look through earnings and perceived quality of its consumer exposure.
  • ⚠️ Analysts have flagged 2 key risks for Berkshire, including pressure on earnings expectations and a recent decline in profit margins, which can limit flexibility if portfolio companies underperform at the same time.
  • 🎁 Abel’s stance underlines Berkshire’s role as a supportive long term owner, which can help portfolio companies focus on customer competitiveness rather than short term financial engineering.
  • 🎁 A more stable relationship with Kraft Heinz can reduce the chance of forced selling or reactive moves around this stake, giving Berkshire more room to apply its patient, low turnover investment style.

What To Watch Going Forward

From here, you may want to watch how Kraft Heinz executes under its unified structure, and whether Berkshire’s management becomes more vocal with other large holdings when big structural moves are on the table. Any future comments from Abel on portfolio companies, as well as updates on Berkshire’s segment level profitability and capital allocation, can give you additional context for how this approach is playing out across the wider group.

data.title: Norfolk Southern Union Pacific Merger Reshapes Rail Network And Risk Profile
data.description:
• Norfolk Southern (NYSE:NSC) has entered into a merger agreement with Union Pacific to form what is described as America's first transcontinental railroad.
  • The transaction comes while Norfolk Southern continues to face legal proceedings related to the 2023 East Palestine incident.
  • These developments are expected to affect the company's operations, regulatory profile, and position within the North American rail industry.

For investors watching Norfolk Southern at a share price of $314.94, this mix of corporate restructuring and legal overhang creates a complex setup. The stock shows returns of 2.8% over the past week and 8.4% over the past month, with 9.4% year to date and 25.5% over 1 year. This gives useful context for how the market has recently treated NYSE:NSC.

Over a longer stretch, Norfolk Southern has posted returns of 47.7% over 3 years and 35.5% over 5 years. These figures offer additional data points as you think about risk and time horizon. As the merger process and legal matters progress, the key questions will center on how the combined rail network is structured, the regulatory conditions attached, and how any East Palestine outcomes affect costs and capital priorities.

Stay updated on the most important news stories for Norfolk Southern by adding it to your watchlist or portfolio. Alternatively, explore our Community to discover new perspectives on Norfolk Southern.

4 things going right for Norfolk Southern that this headline doesn't cover.

The merger agreement with Union Pacific aims to combine two large rail networks into what management describes as America’s first transcontinental railroad, which could reshape how Norfolk Southern serves long-haul freight customers. For you as an investor, the key angle is how efficiently the two systems can be integrated while regulators review the deal and the East Palestine legal process continues. Management has already highlighted potential gains from operational efficiency and broader customer coverage, but there is also execution risk if technology, labor, or regulatory conditions do not line up as planned. At the same time, Norfolk Southern has been focusing on cost discipline, precision scheduled railroading, and infrastructure upgrades, which suggests the company is trying to keep its standalone operations on a firmer footing while the merger path plays out. With sentiment indicators pointing to strong interest in the stock and trading strategies being framed around risk reward setups, this news effectively adds another layer that you need to factor in when thinking about time horizon, position size, and how much regulatory and integration risk you are comfortable carrying.

Advertisement

How This Fits Into The Norfolk Southern Narrative

  • The planned Union Pacific tie up links directly to the earlier narrative around unlocking network value and operational efficiency, and could reinforce efforts to streamline routes and improve service for merchandise and intermodal freight.
  • Regulatory scrutiny of a large rail merger and the ongoing East Palestine legal matters could challenge prior assumptions that cost reduction and PSR 2.0 alone would support earnings, by introducing additional compliance and remediation costs.
  • The narrative discusses weather related costs, coal pricing, and trade policy, but a full transcontinental combination and the associated justice department and Surface Transportation Board reviews may not be fully reflected in those earlier risk scenarios.

Knowing what a company is worth starts with understanding its story. Check out one of the top narratives in the Simply Wall St Community for Norfolk Southern to help decide what it's worth to you.

The Risks and Rewards Investors Should Consider

  • ⚠️ Large rail combinations can attract extended regulatory review and potentially new operating conditions, which may limit how much Norfolk Southern can change pricing, capacity, or competitive behavior versus peers like CSX and Canadian National Railway.
  • ⚠️ The East Palestine legal proceedings and related technology and safety risks could pressure cash flows if settlements, remediation, or mandated investments turn out to be higher than currently expected.
  • 🎁 A combined Norfolk Southern Union Pacific network could create a more seamless coast to coast service, which may appeal to intermodal and industrial customers looking for fewer handoffs and simpler contracts.
  • 🎁 Ongoing work on infrastructure, advanced train control systems, and precision scheduled railroading may help Norfolk Southern extract more value from any combined network and stay competitive with other North American freight railroads.

What To Watch Going Forward

From here, keep an eye on three things. First, how regulators frame any conditions on the merger, including potential asset sales, service requirements, or changes to competitive rules versus CSX, Canadian Pacific Kansas City, and trucking alternatives. Second, updates on East Palestine, particularly around the size and timing of any settlements or required safety investments. Third, management commentary on service quality, cost discipline, and technology rollout, because those operational metrics will influence how much benefit Norfolk Southern might actually capture if the transcontinental combination proceeds. Together, these factors will shape whether the current mix of merger ambition and legal overhang tilts more toward opportunity or risk in your portfolio.

data.title: Is It Time To Reassess IBM (IBM) After The Recent Share Price Pullback?
data.description:
• If you are wondering whether International Business Machines (NYSE:IBM) still offers fair value after a long run in the share price, this article walks through what the numbers currently suggest.
  • IBM closed at US$262.38, with returns of 2.9% over 1 year, a large gain over 3 years and 181.8% over 5 years. However, the stock has seen a 12.2% decline over 7 days, 14.2% over 30 days and a 10.0% decline year to date.
  • Recent headlines have focused on IBM's ongoing push into software and services, along with its role in areas like hybrid cloud and artificial intelligence. These areas continue to shape how investors think about its long term prospects. At the same time, discussion around large technology and software names generally has influenced how the market prices IBM relative to its peers.
  • Our valuation model currently gives IBM a score of 4 out of 6, based on how often it appears undervalued across several checks. Next, we will walk through those methods before finishing with a different way to think about what this valuation really means for you.

International Business Machines delivered 2.9% returns over the last year. See how this stacks up to the rest of the IT industry.

Advertisement

Approach 1: International Business Machines Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model takes projected future cash flows and discounts them back to today to estimate what a business could be worth now. It is essentially asking what future cash generation is worth in today’s dollars.

For International Business Machines, the model uses a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach, starting from last twelve month free cash flow of about $11.2b. Analyst forecasts are used for the first several years, then Simply Wall St extrapolates further cash flows, including a projected free cash flow of $25.6b in 2030. All of these future figures are discounted back to today using the model’s assumptions.

Putting this together, the DCF outputs an estimated intrinsic value of about $401.42 per share. Compared to the recent share price of $262.38, this implies the stock is 34.6% undervalued based on these cash flow projections and discount rate choices.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Our Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) analysis suggests International Business Machines is undervalued by 34.6%. Track this in your watchlist or portfolio, or discover 53 more high quality undervalued stocks.

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for International Business Machines.

Approach 2: International Business Machines Price vs Earnings

For a profitable company like International Business Machines, the P/E ratio is a straightforward way to relate what you pay for each share to the earnings that support it. In general, higher expected earnings growth and lower perceived risk can justify a higher P/E, while lower growth expectations or higher risk tend to align with a lower, more cautious P/E.

IBM currently trades on a P/E of 23.2x. This is in line with the IT industry average P/E of 23.2x and above the peer group average of 18.1x. To go a step further, Simply Wall St also calculates a proprietary “Fair Ratio” of 33.2x, which is the P/E level suggested by factors such as IBM’s earnings profile, industry, profit margins, market cap and identified risks.

This Fair Ratio goes beyond simple peer or industry comparisons because it adjusts for company specific traits like growth expectations, risk and profitability, rather than assuming all IT stocks deserve similar P/E levels. Comparing IBM’s current P/E of 23.2x with the Fair Ratio of 33.2x is used here to suggest the shares may be trading below the level implied by these fundamentals.

Result: UNDERVALUED

P/E ratios tell one story, but what if the real opportunity lies elsewhere? Start investing in legacies, not executives. Discover our 23 top founder-led companies.

Upgrade Your Decision Making: Choose your International Business Machines Narrative

Earlier we mentioned that there is an even better way to understand valuation, so let us introduce you to Narratives. These are simply your own story about a company that ties together what you think its future revenue, earnings and margins could look like, the fair value that falls out of those assumptions, and how that compares with today’s price.

On Simply Wall St’s Community page, Narratives let you plug in a clear view of IBM. For example, this might be a higher fair value such as US$390 based on stronger software and AI execution, or a lower fair value such as US$198 based on tougher competition and slower growth. You can then see how those stories translate into detailed forecasts and a valuation you can compare directly to the current share price to help you decide if IBM looks attractive, fully priced or expensive.

Narratives on the platform are refreshed as new information such as earnings, guidance or news is added. This gives you an easy way to keep your IBM view current without rebuilding your model each time. You can also see how your assumptions line up against other investors, whether they lean toward the more optimistic narrative around AI, hybrid cloud and quantum, the more cautious narrative focused on competitive and balance sheet risks, or something closer to the US$302 consensus in between.

For International Business Machines, however, we will make it really easy for you with previews of two leading International Business Machines Narratives:

🐂 International Business Machines Bull Case

Fair value in this bullish narrative: US$302.05 per share

Implied discount to this fair value vs last close: about 13.1% undervalued

Assumed revenue growth: 5.18% a year

  • Analysts in this camp see IBM’s focus on hybrid cloud, AI and acquisitions like HashiCorp and Confluent as supporting revenue growth, stronger margins and a higher quality mix of recurring software revenue.
  • They build in earnings rising from US$5.9b to US$10.5b by 2028, with profit margins moving toward about 14%, and assume the market is willing to pay a higher future P/E multiple to reflect that earnings profile.
  • Currency swings, competition in software and cloud, and macro related pauses in consulting projects are key watchpoints that could challenge this view if they persist or intensify.

🐻 International Business Machines Bear Case

Fair value in this bearish narrative: US$198.00 per share

Implied premium to this fair value vs last close: about 32.5% overvalued

Assumed revenue growth: 4.60% a year

  • Analysts on the cautious side focus on pressure from larger cloud platforms, the shift to cloud native workloads and ongoing costs tied to debt, compliance and complex operations that could limit IBM’s flexibility.
  • They assume earnings reach about US$8.8b by 2028 with margins closer to 12%, and apply a lower future P/E multiple than the broader US IT group, which pulls their fair value estimate down.
  • This view could be challenged if IBM’s hybrid cloud and AI offerings show stronger traction, free cash flow remains solid and acquisitions and partnerships translate into clearer revenue and margin support.

Stepping back, both narratives use the same raw building blocks, such as revenue growth in the mid single digits and higher margins over time. They place different weight on competition, execution and how generous the future P/E should be. The useful question for you is which set of assumptions feels closer to how you see IBM’s software, AI and quantum efforts playing out, and whether today’s US$262.38 share price looks closer to the bullish US$302 fair value, the cautious US$198 fair value, or somewhere in between once you plug in your own expectations.

  • The stock has been volatile recently, with a 19.8% return over the last 7 days, 6.4% over 30 days, a year-to-date return showing an 8.7% decline, and a 26.4% return over the past year. The 3-year return currently reflects a 12.3% decline.
  • Recent attention on Rivian has centered on its position in the EV space, its production ramp-up and its ability to fund operations. Together, these factors help explain why the share price has been moving around, and they provide useful context for considering whether the current price fairly reflects the company’s prospects or leans too much on optimism or caution.
  • Rivian currently scores 2 out of 6 on our valuation checks, meaning it screens as undervalued on 2 of the 6 measures we track. You can see the details in our valuation score. Next, we will walk through the main valuation approaches before finishing with a look at a more complete way to think about what the stock might be worth.

Rivian Automotive scores just 2/6 on our valuation checks. See what other red flags we found in the full valuation breakdown.

Approach 1: Rivian Automotive Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

The Discounted Cash Flow model estimates what a company might be worth today by taking projected future cash flows and discounting them back to a present value.

For Rivian Automotive, the model used is a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach. The latest twelve month free cash flow is a loss of about US$2.2b. Analysts provide free cash flow estimates out to 2030, and Simply Wall St extrapolates beyond that to build a 10 year view.

Within those projections, free cash flow is forecast to shift from losses in the next few years to a positive US$2.0b in 2030, with further estimated growth after that based on the same framework. All of these future cash flows are discounted back to today and combined into a single estimate.

On this basis, the DCF model suggests an intrinsic value of about US$44.42 per share. This implies the current price is trading at a 60.1% discount to that estimate and indicates a stock that screens as materially undervalued on this model alone.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Our Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) analysis suggests Rivian Automotive is undervalued by 60.1%. Track this in your watchlist or portfolio, or discover 53 more high quality undervalued stocks.

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for Rivian Automotive.

Approach 2: Rivian Automotive Price vs Sales

For companies that are not yet consistently profitable, the P/S ratio is often more useful than P/E because it focuses on revenue rather than earnings, which can still be affected by heavy investment and early stage losses.

In general, higher growth expectations and lower perceived risk tend to support a higher P/S multiple. Slower growth and higher risk usually point to a lower, more conservative range. So what counts as a reasonable P/S depends on what investors think they are paying for in terms of future scale and stability.

Rivian Automotive currently trades on a P/S of 4.08x. That sits above both the Auto industry average of 0.60x and the peer group average of 1.71x, which might suggest the stock carries a richer revenue multiple than many comparables. Simply Wall St also calculates a Fair Ratio of 1.29x, which is an estimate of the P/S you might expect given factors such as Rivian’s earnings profile, margins, industry, market cap and risk characteristics.

The Fair Ratio is designed to be more tailored than a simple peer or industry comparison because it adjusts for growth, risk and profitability differences rather than assuming all companies deserve similar multiples. Comparing Rivian’s current 4.08x P/S to the Fair Ratio of 1.29x points to a valuation that is higher than this model suggests.

Result: OVERVALUED

Upgrade Your Decision Making: Choose your Rivian Automotive Narrative

Earlier we mentioned that there is an even better way to understand valuation. Let us introduce you to Narratives, which are simple stories you create about Rivian Automotive that tie your view of its products, partnerships and risks to specific forecasts for revenue, earnings and margins. These are then linked to a Fair Value you can compare with today’s share price, all within the Simply Wall St Community page that millions of investors use. Narratives update automatically when new news or earnings arrive. You can see, for example, one Rivian Narrative that assumes a Fair Value of about US$10.00 per share and another that assumes around US$19.89, giving you a clear, numbers backed way to decide whether your own view sits closer to the more cautious or more optimistic end of the spectrum.

For Rivian Automotive however we will make it really easy for you with previews of two leading Rivian Automotive Narratives:

🐂 Rivian Automotive Bull Case

Fair value: US$25.41 per share

Implied discount to this fair value: about 30.2% based on the last close of US$17.73

Revenue growth assumption: 48%

  • Sees Gen2 R1T and R1S as a refinement of the current products, with learnings from existing owners and lower build costs feeding into future margins.
  • Leans on the R2, R3 and R3X line up to broaden the customer base, while the Volkswagen joint venture and supplier scale are expected to support software, electrical architecture and cost leverage.
  • Factors in additional contribution from Amazon EDV orders, broader European expansion, third party use of Rivian charging infrastructure and an eventual move toward gross profitability guidance in FY25.

🐻 Rivian Automotive Bear Case

Fair value: US$16.96 per share

Implied premium to this fair value: about 4.5% based on the last close of US$17.73

Revenue growth assumption: 40.76%

  • Builds in strong revenue expectations tied to R2, manufacturing efficiencies and software or services, but also assumes higher future P/E multiples to justify the fair value.
  • Highlights risks around policy changes, cash burn, funding needs, concentration in a relatively narrow product set and intense EV competition, which could pressure margins and delay profitability.
  • Uses the current analyst consensus target of about US$13.85 as an anchor, with the view that at around US$17.73, the share price already sits slightly above this narrative fair value, so outcomes depend heavily on execution against ambitious 2028 revenue and earnings assumptions.

These two narratives frame the current debate clearly. One leans toward Rivian Automotive offering more perceived upside if its product plans and partnerships develop as anticipated. The other treats the stock as closer to fairly priced or slightly rich once execution and funding risks are fully weighed. Your task is to decide which story, if either, aligns best with your own expectations for the business, its balance sheet and your tolerance for volatility.
